critique.summary: The article describes Horizon, a GPU-accelerated terminal emulator built in Rust that offers an infinite canvas for organizing terminal sessions. It features pan and zoom capabilities, workspace grouping, AI agent integration, git status panels, and session persistence.
mainPoints: Horizon provides an infinite 2D canvas for terminal sessions, allowing users to place, resize, and group terminal panels freely.
It supports full terminal emulation with 24-bit color, mouse reporting, scrollback, and uses the Alacritty terminal engine.
The tool integrates AI agents such as Claude Code and Codex, with session persistence and token usage tracking.
Built-in git integration offers real-time git status, inline diffs, and hunk-level details within the terminal environment.
User-friendly features include smart detection of URLs and file paths, a live settings editor with YAML syntax highlighting, and session persistence across restarts.
critique.truthfulness: The claims about Horizon's features are consistent with the information available on its official GitHub repository, which details its capabilities, dependencies, and usage. The repository is active with a clear description of the software's functionality, supported platforms, and installation instructions. The integration of Alacritty as the terminal engine and use of Rust and GPU acceleration are verifiable. However, the article is essentially a project README and does not cite external sources or independent reviews, so while the technical claims appear accurate, they are based on the developer's description without third-party validation.
considerations: The article does not provide independent user reviews or benchmarks to verify performance claims such as speed or GPU acceleration benefits.
There is no discussion of potential limitations, compatibility issues, or system requirements beyond minimal notes.
Security implications of integrating AI agents and session persistence are not addressed.
The article does not compare Horizon to other terminal emulators or workspace management tools to contextualize its advantages or disadvantages.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers around Horizon, a GPU-accelerated infinite-canvas terminal, with users comparing it to existing tools like Ghostty, Tmux, and Niri. Suggestions include improving multi-screen support, zoom capabilities, and persistence features, while some express concerns about usability and interface complexity.

comments.author: tekacs
comments.text:
This is fun! I switched to https://github.com/manaflow-ai/cmux for a while, but had to switch back to Ghostty due to its unreliability, high memory and CPU usage and a bunch of bugs.

This makes a lot of sense, but... it'd be great to allow pulling out of a canvas into a second canvas for those of us with multiple screens (you at least end up needing one window per screen).

In general it feels like... more structure rather than less feels like it'd be the smoothest experience. I'll play with your Ctrl+K shortcut and see if it ends up feeling like I can get everywhere that I need quickly.

But... nice work!

Note for jj users like me: you need to `git lfs pull` if you want to `cargo run --release`!

Update: No luck creating any 'shell' workspaces (it looks like you use GNU-only flags to script) – I'll push a fix once I find it.

Also: the AGENTS.md is wrong JFYI - it points to portable-pty, when this is using alacritty_terminal's tty (on rustix-openpty)
comments.analysis: This comment provides detailed, experience-based feedback with constructive suggestions and points out specific technical issues, making it highly accurate and useful.

comments.author: ms_menardi
comments.text:
This is great! Two things I noticed immediately:

the settings window overlaps with the minimap.

I think it'd be neat if you could zoom? The reason I'd use this over a tiling window manager is to fit more terminals on a screen at once. I think if there was a capability like the Strategic Zoom in Supreme Commander (video: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=9hJY7exr9KU) it would be much easier to manage many terminals at once, and take advantage of the infinite canvas.
comments.analysis: The comment offers practical UI improvement ideas grounded in user experience, enhancing the infinite canvas concept's usability.

comments.author: cedws
comments.text:
Spatial memory is really underutilised in computing.

When libghostty[0] releases maybe you could use that so you don't have to build everything from scratch.

[0]: https://mitchellh.com/writing/libghostty-is-coming
comments.analysis: This comment provides a relevant suggestion that could reduce development effort, showing awareness of related projects.

comments.author: jauntywundrkind
comments.text:
Feels very Niri like, in a positive way. Niri's "scrollable" interface always has room to the right & down!

One of the Niri tools I built for myself is to let me insert a new row up top, to expand up. Thats afaik not super easy in base Niri, constraints the spatial growth some.

I'm pretty close to having some persistence of terminal windows, tmux, etc in Niri, with some tools to dump current state nicely. Doing an actual restore operation would be a good thing to build towards.
comments.analysis: Insightful comparison and feature suggestions that could improve Horizon's functionality, reflecting thoughtful engagement.

comments.author: cadamsdotcom
comments.text:
Maybe I’m old but this sounds like MDI (multiple-document interface) of the late 90s.

Went the way of the dodo probably because it’s hard for non power users to grok.

But this is a power user tool - seeems like a fit!
comments.analysis: Provides historical context and usability perspective, highlighting potential challenges for general users.

comments.author: phrmendes
comments.analysis: A terse comment that dismisses the project without explanation, adding little value to the discussion.

comments.author: hokkos
comments.text: The last thing I want is the figma experience for my terminal
comments.analysis: Expresses a subjective dislike for complex graphical interfaces in terminals, reflecting a valid user preference but lacking constructive input.

comments.author: fritzo
comments.text: I was hoping this would be an infinite length terminal view, like the opening backstory in Star Wars
comments.analysis: Shares a personal expectation that clarifies potential misunderstandings about the project's scope.

comments.author: sakopov
comments.text: Will there be any built releases available?
comments.analysis: A straightforward question about release availability, relevant but not providing analysis or critique.
